questions.question: The diagram shows the net of a closed rectangular box. The dimensions of the faces are $5\text{ cm} \times 4\text{ cm}$, $5\text{ cm} \times 10\text{ cm}$, and $4\text{ cm} \times 10\text{ cm}$. Calculate the volume of the box.
questions.explanation:
The dimensions of the box are $5, 4,$ and $10$. Volume $= 5 \times 4 \times 10 = 200\text{ cm}^3$.

**Common mistake:** A student might add the dimensions ($5+4+10=19$) or calculate the surface area ($220$) instead of volume.

questions.question: A solid prism has a cross-section of a regular hexagon with side $2\text{ cm}$ and area $10.4\text{ cm}^2$. The length of the prism is $8\text{ cm}$. Calculate the total surface area of its net.
questions.explanation:
Total area $= 2 \times BaseArea + Perimeter \times Length$. So $Area = 2(10.4) + (6 \times 2) \times 8 = 20.8 + 96 = 116.8\text{ cm}^2$.

**Common mistake:** A student might forget to include both hexagonal bases or only calculate the area of the rectangles.

questions.question: Hence, given the net of a cylinder where the rectangle has a length of $20\pi\text{ cm}$ and a width of $15\text{ cm}$, find the radius of the circular faces.
questions.explanation:
The length of the rectangle equals the circumference: $2\pi r = 20\pi \Rightarrow 2r = 20 \Rightarrow r = 10\text{ cm}$.

**Common mistake:** The student might assume the radius is half the width ($7.5$) or the full length ($20$).

questions.question: Show that the total surface area of a cube with side length $s$ is $6s^2$. If $s = 0.5\text{ m}$, what is the area of the net in $\text{cm}^2$?
questions.explanation:
$s = 50\text{ cm}$. Area $= 6 \times 50^2 = 6 \times 2500 = 15000\text{ cm}^2$.

**Common mistake:** A student might calculate $6 \times 0.5^2 = 1.5$ and then fail to convert the units correctly (forgetting that $1\text{ m}^2 = 10000\text{ cm}^2$).

questions.question: Find the number of edges on a 3D solid if its net has^8 congruent triangles that meet at a single central point when folded.
questions.explanation:
This describes an octagonal pyramid. It has 8 base edges and 8 slant edges, totaling 16 edges.

**Common mistake:** A student might count only the 8 triangles or confuse it with an octahedron (which has 12 edges).
